title: Decorators & Metaprogramming
subtitle: Java annotations that actually run code

sections.java: // Annotations are metadata — inert by themselves @Override @Deprecated public String legacy() { ... } // A framework must reflectively read them // (Spring, JUnit) for them to DO anything
sections.python: # Decorators ARE functions that wrap functions @cache @log_calls def legacy() -> str: ... # @log_calls actively replaces legacy with # a wrapped version — no framework needed

sections.title: Decorators Run Code; Annotations Are Just Tags
sections.content: Java annotations are passive metadata — a framework (Spring, JUnit) must use reflection to find and act on them. A Python decorator is an active function that takes the decorated function and returns a replacement. @log_calls literally does legacy = log_calls(legacy). The behavior is in the decorator itself, no runtime container required.

sections.java: // Cross-cutting concern via Spring AOP @Cacheable("users") public User findUser(Long id) { return repo.load(id); } // Requires Spring proxying + config
sections.python: # stdlib memoization, zero config from functools import lru_cache @lru_cache(maxsize=128) def find_user(id: int) -> User: return repo.load(id) # Results cached automatically

sections.title: Batteries-Included Decorators
sections.content: The stdlib ships decorators you'd reach for a framework to get in Java: @functools.lru_cache (memoization), @property (computed getter), @staticmethod / @classmethod, @dataclass (Module 06), @functools.wraps (preserve metadata when writing your own). No DI container, no proxying.

sections.java: // Getter/setter pair for a computed value public double getCelsius() { return (fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9; } public void setCelsius(double c) { this.fahrenheit = c * 9 / 5 + 32; }
sections.python: # @property — attribute access, method logic @property def celsius(self) -> float: return (self.fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9 @celsius.setter def celsius(self, c: float): self.fahrenheit = c * 9 / 5 + 32 # Used as: temp.celsius = 25 (no parens!)

sections.title: @property: Public Fields With Hidden Logic
sections.content: Because @property exposes method logic behind plain attribute syntax (temp.celsius, not temp.getCelsius()), Python code starts with public attributes and adds validation/computation later without breaking callers. This is why Python skips getters/setters up front — you can always promote a field to a property with zero call-site changes.

sections.title: Writing Your Own? Use functools.wraps
sections.content: A naive decorator replaces your function with the wrapper, clobbering its __name__, docstring, and signature — breaking introspection and other tooling. Always apply @functools.wraps(func) to the inner wrapper so the metadata is copied through. It's the small ceremony that keeps stack traces and help() meaningful.

tabs.label: Define a decorator
tabs.content:
import functools

def log_calls(func):
    @functools.wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        print(f"→ {func.__name__}{args}")
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f"← {result}")
        return result
    return wrapper

tabs.label: Apply it
tabs.content:
@log_calls
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b

add(2, 3)
# → add(2, 3)
# ← 5

Equivalent to add = log_calls(add) — the decorator just sugars that reassignment.

sections.question: How does a Python decorator fundamentally differ from a Java annotation?
options: Decorators can only be applied to classes
A decorator is a function that actively wraps/replaces the target; an annotation is passive metadata a framework must read reflectively
There is no difference — they're the same feature
Annotations run code but decorators don't
sections.explanation: A Java annotation is inert metadata — nothing happens unless a framework uses reflection to find and act on it. A Python decorator is a higher-order function that receives the decorated object and returns a replacement, so @log_calls immediately rewires behavior with no container or reflection involved.
